narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 71% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. You have solid aspects like being a full-time student from an upper-middle-class family, identifying as a white male—traits that align closely with the majority of applicants who succeed. However, your academic record raised the biggest red flags: your third-year school decile ranking of 3rd place (below the typical 5.5th) was the single most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting failure, as it signals weaker performance compared to peers. Right behind that, your first-year decile of 5th (a bit under the average 5.7th) amplified concerns about inconsistent early grades. Your LSAT score of 31, well below the usual 37, ranked next in influence, suggesting the model sees it as a strong hurdle for bar success. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 (versus the average 3.2) contributed notably, painting a picture of below-average preparation from undergrad. Your family income level nudged the prediction slightly toward risk, even if it's respectable, while your racial background had a minor role since it's the most common among successful test-takers. Less impactful were your gender and full-time status, both typical and not major drivers here. Overall, these top factors—especially your later-year grades, LSAT, and undergrad performance—far outweighed the positives, unlike typical admitted students who boast stronger metrics across the board. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT aiming for 35+, build a higher GPA through grad courses or post-bac programs, and demonstrate grade improvement in any advanced studies. With targeted prep like bar-focused tutoring early on, students like you have turned similar profiles around. Hang in there—this feedback is a roadmap, not a dead end.
